  • 2-2) Brightest Position on Street (LC 2021) — Weighted Sweep Line
  • 2-3) Divide Intervals Into Minimum Number of Groups (LC 2406) — Sweep Line Peak Count
  • 2-4) My Calendar II (LC 731) — Track Double-Booked Intervals

Scanning Line (Line Sweep) Algorithm

Overview

Scanning Line (also known as Line Sweep or Sweep Line) is an algorithmic paradigm that processes geometric objects by imagining a vertical line sweeping across the plane from left to right, processing events as they occur.

Key: transform change to event, so we can handle the changed state via program, instead of dealing with continouous info.

Key Properties

  • Time Complexity: O(n log n) for sorting + O(n) for processing
  • Space Complexity: O(n) for storing events
  • Core Idea: Convert interval problems into event-based processing
  • When to Use: Interval overlaps, skyline problems, calendar conflicts, geometric intersections

Algorithm Principle

  1. Convert intervals into events (start/end points)
  2. Sort events by position (and type if at same position)
  3. Process events in order while maintaining state
  4. Track maximum/minimum or other statistics during sweep

References

  • NTNU Algorithm Notes
  • Line Sweep Tutorial
  • Computational Geometry

Problem Categories

Pattern 1: Interval Overlap

  • Description: Finding maximum overlapping intervals at any point
  • Examples: LC 253, 1094, 2021, 2406, 2848
  • Pattern: Track active intervals using counter

Pattern 2: Skyline Problems

  • Description: Computing visible outline from overlapping rectangles
  • Examples: LC 218, 850, 391
  • Pattern: Process building start/end with heights

Pattern 3: Calendar Booking

  • Description: Managing calendar events and conflicts
  • Examples: LC 729, 731, 732, 1851
  • Pattern: Track booking counts at each time point

Pattern 4: Employee Free Time

  • Description: Finding common free time across schedules
  • Examples: LC 759, 986, 1229
  • Pattern: Merge intervals then find gaps

Pattern 5: Range Updates

  • Description: Applying updates to ranges efficiently
  • Examples: LC 370, 1109, 1893, 2251
  • Pattern: Difference array with sweep line

Pattern 6: Geometric Intersection

  • Description: Finding intersections of geometric objects
  • Examples: LC 836, 223, 391, 850
  • Pattern: Sort by x-coordinate, track y-intervals

Templates & Algorithms

Template Comparison Table

Template Type Use Case Event Types Complexity When to Use
Basic Sweep Count overlaps Start/End O(n log n) Meeting rooms, intervals
Weighted Sweep Sum of overlaps Start/End + value O(n log n) Brightness, bandwidth
Skyline Height tracking Start/End + height O(n log n) Building outline
Difference Array Range updates Update points O(n) Batch updates
Interval Merge Combine intervals Start/End O(n log n) Free time, union
2D Sweep Rectangle area X and Y events O(n² log n) Area calculation

Template 1: Basic Interval Overlap

1# Python - Count maximum overlapping intervals
2def maxOverlap(intervals):
3    events = []
4    
5    # Create events for each interval
6    for start, end in intervals:
7        events.append((start, 1))   # Start event
8        events.append((end, -1))     # End event
9    
10    # Sort events (by time, then by type)
11    events.sort(key=lambda x: (x[0], -x[1]))  # Process start before end at same time
12    
13    # Sweep through events
14    max_overlap = 0
15    current_overlap = 0
16    
17    for time, delta in events:
18        current_overlap += delta
19        max_overlap = max(max_overlap, current_overlap)
20    
21    return max_overlap
22
23# With position tracking
24def maxOverlapPosition(intervals):
25    events = []
26    for start, end in intervals:
27        events.append((start, 1))
28        events.append((end, -1))
29    
30    events.sort(key=lambda x: (x[0], -x[1]))
31    
32    max_overlap = 0
33    max_position = 0
34    current_overlap = 0
35    
36    for time, delta in events:
37        current_overlap += delta
38        if current_overlap > max_overlap:
39            max_overlap = current_overlap
40            max_position = time
41    
42    return max_overlap, max_position

1// Java - Maximum interval overlap
2public int maxOverlap(int[][] intervals) {
3    List<int[]> events = new ArrayList<>();
4    
5    // Create events
6    for (int[] interval : intervals) {
7        events.add(new int[]{interval[0], 1});   // Start
8        events.add(new int[]{interval[1], -1});  // End
9    }
10    
11    // Sort events
12    Collections.sort(events, (a, b) -> {
13        if (a[0] != b[0]) return a[0] - b[0];
14        return b[1] - a[1];  // Start before end
15    });
16    
17    // Sweep
18    int maxOverlap = 0;
19    int currentOverlap = 0;
20    
21    for (int[] event : events) {
22        currentOverlap += event[1];
23        maxOverlap = Math.max(maxOverlap, currentOverlap);
24    }
25    
26    return maxOverlap;
27}

Template 2: Weighted Interval Overlap

1# Python - Sum of overlapping values (e.g., brightness)
2def maxWeightedOverlap(weighted_intervals):
3    events = []
4    
5    # weighted_intervals: [(start, end, weight)]
6    for start, end, weight in weighted_intervals:
7        events.append((start, weight))   # Add weight
8        events.append((end, -weight))    # Remove weight
9    
10    events.sort()
11    
12    max_weight = 0
13    current_weight = 0
14    result_position = 0
15    
16    for position, delta in events:
17        current_weight += delta
18        if current_weight > max_weight:
19            max_weight = current_weight
20            result_position = position
21    
22    return max_weight, result_position
23
24# Track all positions with their weights
25def allWeightedPositions(weighted_intervals):
26    from collections import defaultdict
27    events = defaultdict(int)
28    
29    for start, end, weight in weighted_intervals:
30        events[start] += weight
31        events[end] -= weight
32    
33    sorted_positions = sorted(events.keys())
34    positions_weights = {}
35    current_weight = 0
36    
37    for pos in sorted_positions:
38        current_weight += events[pos]
39        positions_weights[pos] = current_weight
40    
41    return positions_weights

1// Java - Weighted intervals
2public int maxWeightedOverlap(int[][] weightedIntervals) {
3    // weightedIntervals: [start, end, weight]
4    TreeMap<Integer, Integer> events = new TreeMap<>();
5    
6    for (int[] interval : weightedIntervals) {
7        events.put(interval[0], 
8                  events.getOrDefault(interval[0], 0) + interval[2]);
9        events.put(interval[1], 
10                  events.getOrDefault(interval[1], 0) - interval[2]);
11    }
12    
13    int maxWeight = 0;
14    int currentWeight = 0;
15    
16    for (int delta : events.values()) {
17        currentWeight += delta;
18        maxWeight = Math.max(maxWeight, currentWeight);
19    }
20    
21    return maxWeight;
22}

Template 3: Skyline Problem

1# Python - Building skyline
2def getSkyline(buildings):
3    events = []
4    
5    # buildings: [[left, right, height]]
6    for left, right, height in buildings:
7        events.append((left, -height))  # Start (negative for max heap)
8        events.append((right, height))  # End
9    
10    events.sort(key=lambda x: (x[0], x[1]))
11    
12    result = []
13    heights = [0]  # Ground level
14    
15    import heapq
16    for x, h in events:
17        if h < 0:  # Building start
18            heapq.heappush(heights, h)
19        else:  # Building end
20            heights.remove(-h)
21            heapq.heapify(heights)
22        
23        # Check if max height changed
24        max_h = -heights[0]
25        if not result or result[-1][1] != max_h:
26            result.append([x, max_h])
27    
28    return result

1// Java - Skyline
2public List<List<Integer>> getSkyline(int[][] buildings) {
3    List<int[]> events = new ArrayList<>();
4    
5    for (int[] b : buildings) {
6        events.add(new int[]{b[0], -b[2]});  // Start
7        events.add(new int[]{b[1], b[2]});   // End
8    }
9    
10    Collections.sort(events, (a, b) -> {
11        if (a[0] != b[0]) return a[0] - b[0];
12        return a[1] - b[1];
13    });
14    
15    List<List<Integer>> result = new ArrayList<>();
16    TreeMap<Integer, Integer> heights = new TreeMap<>();
17    heights.put(0, 1);  // Ground
18    
19    for (int[] event : events) {
20        int x = event[0], h = event[1];
21        
22        if (h < 0) {  // Start
23            heights.put(-h, heights.getOrDefault(-h, 0) + 1);
24        } else {  // End
25            if (heights.get(h) == 1) {
26                heights.remove(h);
27            } else {
28                heights.put(h, heights.get(h) - 1);
29            }
30        }
31        
32        int maxH = heights.lastKey();
33        if (result.isEmpty() || 
34            result.get(result.size() - 1).get(1) != maxH) {
35            result.add(Arrays.asList(x, maxH));
36        }
37    }
38    
39    return result;
40}

Template 4: Calendar Booking

1# Python - Calendar with multiple bookings
2class MyCalendarTwo:
3    def __init__(self):
4        self.events = []  # List of (time, delta)
5    
6    def book(self, start, end):
7        # Temporarily add new booking
8        self.events.append((start, 1))
9        self.events.append((end, -1))
10        self.events.sort()
11        
12        # Check if triple booking
13        booked = 0
14        for time, delta in self.events:
15            booked += delta
16            if booked >= 3:
17                # Remove the temporary booking
18                self.events.remove((start, 1))
19                self.events.remove((end, -1))
20                return False
21        
22        return True

1// Java - Calendar booking
2class MyCalendarTwo {
3    List<int[]> events;
4    
5    public MyCalendarTwo() {
6        events = new ArrayList<>();
7    }
8    
9    public boolean book(int start, int end) {
10        events.add(new int[]{start, 1});
11        events.add(new int[]{end, -1});
12        
13        Collections.sort(events, (a, b) -> {
14            if (a[0] != b[0]) return a[0] - b[0];
15            return a[1] - b[1];
16        });
17        
18        int booked = 0;
19        for (int[] event : events) {
20            booked += event[1];
21            if (booked >= 3) {
22                events.remove(new int[]{start, 1});
23                events.remove(new int[]{end, -1});
24                return false;
25            }
26        }
27        
28        return true;
29    }
30}

Template 5: Difference Array Pattern

1# Python - Range addition using sweep line
2def rangeAddition(length, updates):
3    # updates: [[start, end, inc]]
4    diff = [0] * (length + 1)
5    
6    for start, end, inc in updates:
7        diff[start] += inc
8        diff[end + 1] -= inc
9    
10    # Sweep to get final values
11    result = [0] * length
12    current = 0
13    for i in range(length):
14        current += diff[i]
15        result[i] = current
16    
17    return result
18
19# 2D range addition
20def rangeAddition2D(m, n, updates):
21    diff = [[0] * (n + 1) for _ in range(m + 1)]
22    
23    for r1, c1, r2, c2, inc in updates:
24        diff[r1][c1] += inc
25        diff[r1][c2 + 1] -= inc
26        diff[r2 + 1][c1] -= inc
27        diff[r2 + 1][c2 + 1] += inc
28    
29    # 2D prefix sum
30    result = [[0] * n for _ in range(m)]
31    for i in range(m):
32        for j in range(n):
33            result[i][j] = diff[i][j]
34            if i > 0:
35                result[i][j] += result[i-1][j]
36            if j > 0:
37                result[i][j] += result[i][j-1]
38            if i > 0 and j > 0:
39                result[i][j] -= result[i-1][j-1]
40    
41    return result

Template 6: Interval Merge with Sweep

1# Python - Merge overlapping intervals using sweep
2def mergeIntervals(intervals):
3    if not intervals:
4        return []
5    
6    events = []
7    for start, end in intervals:
8        events.append((start, 1))
9        events.append((end, -1))
10    
11    events.sort(key=lambda x: (x[0], -x[1]))
12    
13    merged = []
14    active = 0
15    start = 0
16    
17    for time, delta in events:
18        if active == 0 and delta == 1:
19            start = time  # New interval starts
20        
21        active += delta
22        
23        if active == 0:  # Interval ends
24            merged.append([start, time])
25    
26    return merged

Problems by Pattern

Pattern-Based Problem Tables

Interval Overlap Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Meeting Rooms II 253 Basic sweep line Medium
Car Pooling 1094 Capacity tracking Medium
Brightest Position on Street 2021 Weighted intervals Medium
Maximum Population Year 1854 Year range counting Easy
Maximum Sum Obtained 2848 Points on line Medium
Describe the Painting 1943 Segment merging Medium
Divide Intervals Into Minimum Number of Groups 2406 Event sweep, max concurrent overlaps Medium

Skyline Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
The Skyline Problem 218 Height tracking Hard
Rectangle Area II 850 2D sweep Hard
Perfect Rectangle 391 Corner counting Hard
Falling Squares 699 Segment tree + sweep Hard

Calendar Booking Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
My Calendar I 729 No overlap check Medium
My Calendar II 731 Double booking Medium
My Calendar III 732 K-booking Hard
Minimum Interval to Include Query 1851 Query + sweep Hard

Employee Schedule Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Employee Free Time 759 Interval gaps Hard
Interval List Intersections 986 Two pointers Medium
Meeting Scheduler 1229 Common slots Medium
Remove Covered Intervals 1288 Sorting + sweep Medium

Range Update Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Range Addition 370 Difference array Medium
Corporate Flight Bookings 1109 Difference array Medium
Plates Between Candles 2055 Prefix + binary search Medium
Count Integers in Intervals 2276 Interval merge Hard

Geometric Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Rectangle Overlap 836 2D overlap Easy
Rectangle Area 223 Area calculation Medium
Number of Airplanes in Sky 391 Time points Medium
Line Reflection 356 Coordinate mapping Medium

Pattern Selection Strategy

1Problem Analysis Flowchart:
2
31. Are you counting overlapping intervals?
4   ├── YES → Use Basic Sweep Line
5   │         ├── Fixed capacity? → Track current count
6   │         └── Variable weight? → Track weighted sum
7   └── NO → Continue to 2
8
92. Is it about building heights/skyline?
10   ├── YES → Use Skyline Template
11   │         ├── 1D skyline → Height events
12   │         └── 2D rectangles → Coordinate compression
13   └── NO → Continue to 3
14
153. Managing calendar/bookings?
16   ├── YES → Use Calendar Template
17   │         ├── Single booking → Simple overlap
18   │         ├── Double booking → Count = 2 check
19   │         └── K-booking → Count = K check
20   └── NO → Continue to 4
21
224. Finding free time/gaps?
23   ├── YES → Use Interval Merge
24   │         ├── Merge all intervals
25   │         └── Find gaps between merged
26   └── NO → Continue to 5
27
285. Batch range updates?
29   ├── YES → Use Difference Array
30   │         ├── 1D ranges → Simple difference
31   │         └── 2D ranges → 2D difference
32   └── NO → Use appropriate combination

Summary & Quick Reference

Complexity Quick Reference

Operation Time Complexity Space Notes
Event Creation O(n) O(n) 2 events per interval
Event Sorting O(n log n) O(1) Dominant operation
Sweep Processing O(n) O(1) Single pass
With TreeMap/Heap O(n log n) O(n) For skyline problems
Difference Array O(n + m) O(m) m = range size
2D Sweep O(n² log n) O(n²) Rectangle problems

Template Quick Reference

Template Pattern Key Code
Basic Sweep Count overlaps events.sort(); count += delta
Weighted Sum values weight += delta * value
Skyline Track heights heapq for max height
Calendar Booking conflicts if count >= k: reject
Difference Range updates diff[start]++; diff[end+1]--
Merge Combine intervals if active==0: new interval

Common Patterns & Tricks

Event Ordering Rule

1# Critical: Handle events at same position correctly
2# Start before End at same position
3events.sort(key=lambda x: (x[0], -x[1]))
4# OR End before Start (depends on problem)
5events.sort(key=lambda x: (x[0], x[1]))

Interval to Events Conversion

1# Standard conversion
2for start, end in intervals:
3    events.append((start, +1))  # Enter
4    events.append((end, -1))     # Exit
5    
6# Inclusive vs Exclusive endpoints
7events.append((end, -1))     # Exclusive end
8events.append((end+1, -1))   # Inclusive end

Maximum Tracking Pattern

1max_value = 0
2current = 0
3max_position = 0
4
5for pos, delta in events:
6    current += delta
7    if current > max_value:
8        max_value = current
9        max_position = pos

Skyline Height Management

1# Use negative for max heap in Python
2import heapq
3heights = [0]  # Ground level
4heapq.heappush(heights, -height)  # Add
5max_height = -heights[0]           # Get max

Problem-Solving Steps

  1. Identify Event Types

    • What marks the start of an interval?
    • What marks the end?
    • Are there other event types?

  2. Design Event Structure

    • Position/time
    • Event type (start/end)
    • Additional data (value, id, etc.)

  3. Determine Sort Order

    • Primary: By position/time
    • Secondary: Start vs End handling
    • Tertiary: By value if needed

  4. Process Events

    • Maintain running state
    • Update maximum/minimum
    • Check constraints

  5. Handle Edge Cases

    • Same position events
    • Empty intervals
    • Single point intervals
    • Overlapping endpoints

Common Mistakes & Tips

🚫 Common Mistakes:

  • Wrong event ordering at same position
  • Off-by-one errors with inclusive/exclusive ends
  • Not handling empty interval list
  • Forgetting to track position of maximum
  • Using wrong data structure for height tracking

✅ Best Practices:

  • Always clarify inclusive vs exclusive intervals
  • Use TreeMap/TreeSet for dynamic height queries
  • Consider difference array for range updates
  • Test with overlapping endpoints
  • Visualize the sweep line movement

Interview Tips

  1. Problem Recognition

    • "Maximum overlapping" → Sweep line
    • "Skyline/outline" → Height tracking
    • "Free time" → Merge then find gaps
    • "Range updates" → Difference array

  2. Clarify Requirements

    • Are intervals inclusive or exclusive?
    • Can intervals have zero length?
    • How to handle same-position events?
    • Is the answer count or specific intervals?

  3. Optimization Opportunities

    • Coordinate compression for large ranges
    • Segment tree for dynamic updates
    • Binary search for point queries
    • Lazy propagation for range updates

  4. Common Follow-ups

    • Handle dynamic interval additions
    • Query at specific points
    • Find k-th largest overlap
    • Support interval modifications

Advanced Techniques

Coordinate Compression

1# Compress large coordinate space
2coords = set()
3for start, end in intervals:
4    coords.add(start)
5    coords.add(end)
6coord_map = {v: i for i, v in enumerate(sorted(coords))}

Segment Tree Integration

  • Use for dynamic updates
  • Query range maximum/minimum
  • Lazy propagation for efficiency

Persistent Data Structure

  • Track history of changes
  • Query at any timestamp
  • Useful for temporal databases

Related Topics

  • Interval Problems: Merge, insert, remove intervals
  • Greedy Algorithms: Activity selection
  • Computational Geometry: Line intersection
  • Data Stream: Processing events in order
  • Difference Array: Efficient range updates

LC Examples

2-1) Meeting Rooms II (LC 253) — Sweep Line Peak Count

Emit +1 on start, -1 on end; sort events; peak concurrent count = min rooms needed.

1// LC 253 - Meeting Rooms II
2// IDEA: Sweep line — +1 on start, -1 on end; sort (end before start at ties); track peak
3// time = O(N log N), space = O(N)
4public int minMeetingRooms(int[][] intervals) {
5    List<int[]> events = new ArrayList<>();
6    for (int[] inv : intervals) {
7        events.add(new int[]{inv[0], 1});
8        events.add(new int[]{inv[1], -1});
9    }
10    events.sort((a, b) -> a[0] != b[0] ? a[0] - b[0] : a[1] - b[1]); // end before start at same time
11    int rooms = 0, maxRooms = 0;
12    for (int[] e : events) { rooms += e[1]; maxRooms = Math.max(maxRooms, rooms); }
13    return maxRooms;
14}

1# LC 253 Meeting Rooms II
2# NOTE : there're also priority queue, sorting approaches
3
4# V0
5# IDEA : SCANNING LINE : Sort all time points and label the start and end points. Move a vertical line from left to right.
6class Solution:
7     def minMeetingRooms(self, intervals):
8            lst = []
9            """
10            NOTE THIS !!!
11            """
12            for start, end in intervals:
13                lst.append((start, 1))
14                lst.append((end, -1))
15            # all of below sort work
16            #lst.sort()
17            lst.sort(key = lambda x : [x[0], x[1]])
18            res, curr_rooms = 0, 0
19            for t, n in lst:
20                curr_rooms += n
21                res = max(res, curr_rooms)
22            return res
23
24# V0''
25# IDEA : SCANNING LINE
26# Step 1 : split intervals to points, and label start, end point
27# Step 2 : reorder the points
28# Step 3 : go through every point, if start : result + 1, if end : result -1, and record the maximum result in every iteration
29class Solution:
30    def minMeetingRooms(self, intervals):
31        if intervals is None or len(intervals) == 0:
32            return 0
33
34        tmp = []
35
36        # set up start and end points 
37        for inter in intervals:
38            tmp.append((inter[0], True))
39            tmp.append((inter[1], False))
40
41        # sort 
42        tmp = sorted(tmp, key=lambda v: (v[0], v[1]))
43
44        n = 0
45        max_num = 0
46        for arr in tmp:
47            # start point +1 
48            if arr[1]:
49                n += 1
50            # end point -1 
51            else:
52                n -= 1 # release the meeting room
53            max_num = max(n, max_num)
54        return max_num

2-2) Brightest Position on Street (LC 2021) — Weighted Sweep Line

Emit +1 at p−r and −1 at p+r+1; track position with maximum accumulated brightness.

1// LC 2021 - Brightest Position on Street
2// IDEA: Sweep line — +1 at range start, -1 at range end+1; track max brightness position
3// time = O(N log N), space = O(N)
4public int brightestPosition(int[][] lights) {
5    List<int[]> events = new ArrayList<>();
6    for (int[] light : lights) {
7        events.add(new int[]{light[0] - light[1], 1});
8        events.add(new int[]{light[0] + light[1] + 1, -1});
9    }
10    events.sort((a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]);
11    int brightness = 0, maxBrightness = 0, ans = 0;
12    for (int[] e : events) {
13        brightness += e[1];
14        if (brightness > maxBrightness) { maxBrightness = brightness; ans = e[0]; }
15    }
16    return ans;
17}

1# LC 2021. Brightest Position on Street
2# V0
3# IDEA : Scanning line, LC 253 MEETING ROOM II
4class Solution:
5    def brightestPosition(self, lights: List[List[int]]) -> int:
6        # light range array
7        light_r = []
8        for p,r in lights:
9            light_r.append((p-r,'start'))
10            light_r.append((p+r+1,'end'))
11        light_r.sort(key = lambda x:x[0])
12        # focus on the boundary of light range 
13        
14        bright = collections.defaultdict(int)
15        power = 0
16        for l in light_r:
17            if 'start' in l:
18                power += 1
19            else:
20                power -= 1
21            bright[l[0]] = power # NOTE : we update "power" in each iteration
22                
23        list_bright = list(bright.values())
24        list_position = list(bright.keys())
25        
26        max_bright = max(list_bright)
27        max_bright_index = list_bright.index(max_bright)
28        
29        return list_position[max_bright_index]
30
31# V0'
32# IDEA : Scanning line, meeting room
33from collections import defaultdict
34class Solution(object):
35    def brightestPosition(self, lights):
36        # edge case
37        if not lights:
38            return
39        _lights = []
40        for x in lights:
41            """
42            NOTE this !!!
43             -> 1) scanning line trick
44             -> 2) we add 1 to idx for close session (_lights.append([x[0]+x[1]+1, -1]))
45            """
46            _lights.append([x[0]-x[1], 1])
47            _lights.append([x[0]+x[1]+1, -1])
48        _lights.sort(key = lambda x : x)
49        #print ("_lights = " + str(_lights))
50        d = defaultdict(int)
51        up = 0
52        for a, b in _lights:
53            if b == 1:
54                up += 1
55            else:
56                up -= 1
57            d[a] = up
58        print ("d = " + str(d))
59        _max = max(d.values())
60        res = [i for i in d if d[i] == _max]
61        #print ("res = " + str(res))
62        return min (res)
63
64# V1
65# IDEA : LC 253 MEETING ROOM II
66# https://leetcode.com/problems/brightest-position-on-street/discuss/1494005/Python%3A-Basically-meeting-room-II
67# IDEA :
68# So, the only difference in this problem in comparison to meeting room II is that we have to convert our input into intervals, which is straightforward and basically suggested to use by the first example. So, here is my code and here is meeting rooms II https://leetcode.com/problems/meeting-rooms-ii/
69class Solution:
70    def brightestPosition(self, lights: List[List[int]]) -> int:
71        intervals, heap, res, best = [], [], 0, 0
72        for x, y in lights:
73            intervals.append([x-y, x+y])
74            
75        intervals.sort()
76
77        for left, right in intervals:            
78            while heap and heap[0] < left: 
79                heappop(heap)
80            heappush(heap, right)
81            if len(heap) > best:
82                best = len(heap)
83                res = left
84        return res

2-3) Divide Intervals Into Minimum Number of Groups (LC 2406) — Sweep Line Peak Count

Min groups = peak concurrent overlaps. Three patterns: (1) sweep line events (+1/-1), (2) sort+PQ reuse group when pq.peek() < start (strict < because endpoints are inclusive), (3) difference array for fixed-range coordinates.

Key trap: [1,5] and [5,10] overlap — use pq.peek() < start (not <=) in PQ approach, and sort start(+1) before end(−1) at same time in sweep approach.

Similar LC: 253 Meeting Rooms II, 1094 Car Pooling, 2021 Brightest Position on Street, 1854 Maximum Population Year, 729/731/732 My Calendar I/II/III

1// LC 2406 - Divide Intervals Into Minimum Number of Groups
2// IDEA: Sweep line — +1 on start, -1 on end; start before end at same time (inclusive overlap)
3// time = O(N log N), space = O(N)
4public int minGroups(int[][] intervals) {
5    List<int[]> events = new ArrayList<>();
6    for (int[] inv : intervals) {
7        events.add(new int[]{inv[0], 1});
8        events.add(new int[]{inv[1], -1});
9    }
10    events.sort((a, b) -> a[0] != b[0] ? a[0] - b[0] : b[1] - a[1]); // start(+1) before end(-1)
11    int cur = 0, max = 0;
12    for (int[] e : events) { cur += e[1]; max = Math.max(max, cur); }
13    return max;
14}
15
16// Alt: Sort + Min-PQ (reuse group when earliest end < current start)
17// time = O(N log N), space = O(N)
18public int minGroups_pq(int[][] intervals) {
19    Arrays.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]);
20    PriorityQueue<Integer> pq = new PriorityQueue<>(); // stores end times
21    for (int[] inv : intervals) {
22        if (!pq.isEmpty() && pq.peek() < inv[0]) pq.poll(); // reuse group
23        pq.add(inv[1]);
24    }
25    return pq.size(); // active groups = peak overlap
26}
27
28// Alt: Difference array (fixed coordinate range)
29// time = O(N + M), space = O(M)  where M = max coordinate
30public int minGroups_diff(int[][] intervals) {
31    int[] diff = new int[1_000_002];
32    for (int[] inv : intervals) { diff[inv[0]]++; diff[inv[1] + 1]--; }
33    int max = 0, cur = 0;
34    for (int d : diff) { cur += d; max = Math.max(max, cur); }
35    return max;
36}

2-4) My Calendar II (LC 731) — Track Double-Booked Intervals

New booking is invalid only if it overlaps a double-booked segment; otherwise record overlap.

1// LC 731 - My Calendar II
2// IDEA: Track booked and overlaps lists; reject if new booking intersects any overlap
3// time = O(N^2), space = O(N)
4class MyCalendarTwo {
5    List<int[]> booked = new ArrayList<>(), overlaps = new ArrayList<>();
6    public boolean book(int start, int end) {
7        for (int[] ov : overlaps)
8            if (start < ov[1] && end > ov[0]) return false; // triple overlap
9        for (int[] bk : booked)
10            if (start < bk[1] && end > bk[0])
11                overlaps.add(new int[]{Math.max(start, bk[0]), Math.min(end, bk[1])});
12        booked.add(new int[]{start, end});
13        return true;
14    }
15}
